findings.description: run_with_retries resets `attempt = 0` (line 69) every iteration before incrementing it (line 71), so the counter oscillates 0->1->0->1 and never reaches MAX_RETRIES; any persistently-failing job loops forever, and since this runs synchronously from both poll_loop (line 81) and drain_sync (line 97) it wedges the entire worker (queue stops draining, `failed` never increments, process hangs).

Backoff sleep `RETRY_BACKOFF_SECONDS * (attempt + 1)` is evaluated while attempt is still 0 (incremented on the next line, 71), so every retry sleeps a constant 2.0s; the intended exponential/incremental backoff never occurs and this bug survives any naive fix to the counter reset.

poll_loop is declared `async def` (line 74) but uses blocking `time.sleep` (line 79) instead of `await asyncio.sleep`, and calls the fully-synchronous run_with_retries (line 81); it contains no `await` at all, so on the service event loop it freezes every other coroutine for the full poll interval and retry/subprocess durations -- the async contract is not honored (asyncio imported at line 3 but never awaited).

aggregate computes `sum(job.payload.get("values", []))` with no validation that `values` is a list of numbers; a non-numeric or non-list payload raises TypeError, which is caught at line 67 and -- because of the line-69 counter bug -- turns a malformed job into an infinite loop instead of a clean permanent failure.

convert builds a shell command via f-string from untrusted job.payload source/dest and runs it with `subprocess.run(..., shell=True)` (line 52), a classic shell-injection sink; flagged in passing per logic-subagent scope -- detailed exploit analysis deferred to critic-security.

On a non-zero `convert` exit, execute_job returns False but discards the captured result.stderr (captured at line 52), so operators get no diagnostic about why the conversion failed; minor observability gap, compounded by the fact that the eventual 'failed permanently' log (line 86) is never reached due to the infinite-loop bug.

JobQueue.pop uses `list.pop(0)`, which is O(n) per dequeue (shifts all remaining elements); collections.deque would be O(1). Performance-class, deferred to critic-performance; noted only for full coverage.
